speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Paul Oppenheimer in Solihull, England. This is of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we've now reached after the war and after the liberation. And this is when we returned to Holland, when we arrived in Holland at Maastricht, where we were confronted by the authorities, administrations, and, in particular, the AEF, the Allied Expeditionary Force. And we were called DPs, displaced persons. And this is our registration record-- the original, as you can see. And this is my name, Oppenheimer, Paul-- no [? Friederike-- ?]. Birthdate, Berlin. This was the address of the nearest relatives that we were aware of in London. And, for example, it says Jewish prisoner, Bergen-Belsen there. so that's one of the few official documents that we have to prove that we were in Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there's Maastricht. Perhaps I could just show you the other side. The other side shows various stamps of authorities and dates-- 12th of July, 1945, Maastricht. And it doesn't mention here, this other camp, [INAUDIBLE], but I thought-- I can't see it here, anyway. And it shows that we complied with all the various medical clearance certificates. That we were DDT, it says somewhere there. There it shows you that we were sprayed with DDT and all sorts of other things. [INAUDIBLE] The next thing is there-- oh, types. Immunization, et cetera. Anyway, we had a pretty thorough medical examination to show that we were all right after our experiences in the camp and the typhus and the lice, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is where it actually shows that we were registered in Laren on the 12th of July, '45, is it? Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the earlier episodes in Maastricht were obviously earlier, at that date up here, 28th of June, 1945. So this is where we were several days in the camp with the SS, for Germans. And then we released to the orphanage in Laren, the Bergstichting. And there we had to register with the police, and that kind of confirms it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is our first kiddush, Friday night ceremony, perhaps in Maastricht-- probably in Maastricht. This is Mr. Birnbaum and Mrs. Birnbaum. They were the ones who looked after the orphans from Belsen and on the train. They looked after Eve, in particular, although she's not on this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had six children of their own, one of whom is to the left of Mr. Birnbaum. The boy to the left-- very left-- is Walter [? Kugler, ?] now called Walter Kingsley, living in London, but he doesn't want to know anything about the Holocaust on the last time we contacted him, which was about 1946. He didn't want to know about it. We are the two boys on the right-- me at the top with my cap, and [INAUDIBLE] below, with his Russian officer's hat, a prized possession-- his fur hat that he stole off a dead Russian officer. This is a photograph that was taken with friends of my parents called the [? Haas ?] family. And we're the two boys at the front. Obviously quite happy to be back with normal people and in civilization. This was during our stay at the orphanage in Laren, which is very near to where these people lived in Hilversum. Me on the left, [INAUDIBLE] on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the approximate year-- this was '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. During the summer of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which are the friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's Mr. Haas, and that's his wife, Mrs. Haas. And that's a lady that always lived there. I never quite understood the relationship. And I don't know who the other three people were. I think one of them was a Canadian soldier who was courting, perhaps, that lady, or something like that. I didn't really quite understand the relationships. But the Haas were friends of my parents, and also friends of [? Lotta ?] [? and Rudi ?] in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we're finally allowed to travel from Holland to England-- we had our visa to come to England-- we had to get a Dutch identity certificate, like a travel document, and this is it. That's my photograph. That's my signature, my name, et cetera. And that was a travel document that we used to travel from Holland. May I just turn over? So this was the visa in this document, issued on the 21st of November, 1945 by the British Embassy in Holland, paid for by the [? stamps. ?] And this is when we arrived in England, on the 23rd of November, 1945-- stamp and more stamps and various signatures. So that's when we arrived in England-- 23rd of November, 1945, seven months after our liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my first British travel document. It was not really a passport because I wasn't British at that time. And it's dubious whether I was German or stateless. But this enabled me to travel throughout Europe. And it gives my name on the previous page. There is my birthplace, Berlin, the date. Tool maker trainee, living in Handsworth, Birmingham. Later cancelled when I got an official passport when I became naturalized. Oh, that's another document I should have brought. Photograph of me at that time, which would be '46, something like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in this document, there are various times of places that we went to visit-- Holland, Switzerland, France, et cetera. This is a record that we've handed in at Yad Vashem for the death of my two parents and my four grandparents. So this is the example of the page for my father. And it gives his name, date of birth, that he was killed, died in Bergen-Belsen. And that it was submitted by me in October, 1978. The intention is that all the people killed in the Holocaust should be recorded with a page like that at Yad Vashem. And we've done our duty. This is a photograph of Corrine and myself getting married at the register office in Birmingham on the 26th of March, 1964. And there are various friends and relatives. There are a lot more all around, but they're not on the photograph. This was on our wedding day, just after our wedding, moving into our new, semi-detached house in Alton, Solihull, not very far from here. You will see, on the left-hand side, uncle [? Rudi, ?] me, Aunt [? Lotta, ?] and Eve. And on the other photograph, [INAUDIBLE], me, and Eve, the three survivors from Belsen and the train-- all happy and smiling. This is a photograph of the happy family, taken in 1971, some time in the summer, by the looks of it. Maybe in Wales, where we often went for our holidays, South Wales. There's Corrine and myself. And from the right-- Judith, about one year old or less. Then Nicholas with his big kibbutznik hat. And Simon, on the left, struggling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it possible it could have been taken in Spain? [? CO: It was ?] Spain, Paul. It is possible. [? CO: It ?] was Spain. Was it? All right. I stand corrected. It was in Spain. We did, in those early days, go to Majorca, and maybe it was in Spain. Why? Because of the flag over there? Why do you think it was Spain? So these were our three children in 1974, when Judith was approximately 4. Simon was 7 and Nicholas, 9 Both boys-- taken in our back garden. And the two boys in their school uniform for Rockley Infant School.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the happy family outside Buckingham Palace after the investiture, where I received my MBE medal, carelessly in my right hand. From the left, this is Simon, who unfortunately couldn't come in with us, and he waited for us outside in the rain. Corrine, in her new outfit, specially for the queen. Me, in morning dress. Judith, elegant, and Nicholas, suave. This is the MBE. This is the actual medal, which Her Majesty the Queen lobbed onto me graciously.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what sort of occasion might you wear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're only allowed to wear it-- there are some very strict rules with it. Only allowed to wear it when royalty is present. All various other specific ceremonies. The only times that I ever wear it is for the Remembrance Day Parade, which we have here in Solihull. So that's when everybody wears their war medals, and I wear this one. But I've never been to any kind of parties where any of the royals were present, so where I was allowed to where it. There are a lot of rules about it. For example, it's not returnable on death, which is good to know. So one of you kids can inherit it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the citation that goes with the MBE medal. You can see the top right-hand side, Elizabeth R-- Elizabeth II there. I like the language. 'The Order of the British Empire to our trusty and well-beloved Paul Oppenheimer, Esquire.' Greeting, et cetera. You can read it-- very nice language.

speaker: interviewee
text: Signed by Prince Philip of Edinburgh, Grand Master of the order of the British Empire. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My children are allowed to be married in St. Paul's. I'm not sure about bar mitzvahs. [LAUGHTER] This was when we returned to Belsen in April, 1990. You will see the various monuments there and the gravestones. And on the left top, for example, is [INAUDIBLE] signing in for the visitors book when we go to the documentation center afterwards. Further along, to the right, there's Judith and me by the Jewish memorial at Belsen. And further to the right again, [INAUDIBLE] and me discussing, in the glass cage, a model of Belsen, and trying to figure out exactly where our barracks was because it's difficult to find nowadays. The place is completely different. Everything in Belsen was raised to the ground because of the typhus epidemic with flame throwers, so there's absolutely nothing left from the original camp. Some of the other pictures. This was many of the Israelis amongst the crowd of about 2,000 people that were there on that day to commemorate the liberation of Belsen. And it coincided with the day of the Holocaust, Yam Hashoah, in April, 1990. Another picture of the crowd and various dignitaries who spoke there on that day and who laid wreath. We are [? somewhere. ?] I think that's me there, I think. As I say, Judith was with me, and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Another picture of the crowd. There's Judith. If you point, here. Which is Judith on the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I thought it was that, but no, it isn't. Looking at it, I don't know. It isn't Judith, no. That is a view through the woods in the heathland area towards the obelisk, which is the main feature of the memorial site these days. And behind that is a big wall with many inscriptions in many different languages. That was erected very soon after the war, 1946. These were some of the very few individual burial places. Most of them are mass graves, really. There's Judith, me, [INAUDIBLE]. And this is one of the mass graves, of which there are about a dozen or so. Big mounds in which all the bodies were bulldozed into and then covered over. And here, there is a stone that says, 'Here rest 5,000 dead,' or 'Here rest 9,000 dead,' or 'Here rest 3,000 dead.' When we were entering the camp that day for the ceremonies, everybody, every person was given a single tulip, and everybody laid their tulips on the various graves, like along there. When we were in Belsen in the 1990 for the ceremonies, we noticed that there were a number of individual gravestones, obviously not marking the actual place where people were buried, but a symbolic gravestone to say that there were individual people there who died in Belsen. So we decided to do this, as well. And in 1991, we had this gravestone erected in Belsen in memory of our mother, Friederike and our father, Johann Felix Robert Oppenheimer. It's very close to the Jewish memorial that you saw earlier. And behind it there is one of these being mounds where thousands and thousands of bodies were buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back that year to see the gravestone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were not there that year. We went back two years later. I think in '93 we went back and we saw her gravestone there. And we saw it again in 1995. And you saw it also in 1995.
